narrative: As a Black male applicant with an upper-middle-class family background and plans to attend law school full-time, your profile stood out in several ways, including your undergraduate GPA of 2.20—below the typical 3.21 average—and an LSAT score of 24.50, well under the dataset average of 36.70. The admissions model, trained on past students' data to predict first-time bar exam success, estimated an 80% chance that you would fail the bar exam, which directly led to the rejection of your application since the school prioritizes candidates likely to pass on their first try. This prediction wasn't random; it hinged heavily on three key factors from your record, ranked by their influence on pushing the outcome toward failure. The strongest driver was your Year 3 school decile ranking of 1, placing you in the bottom tenth of your peers—far below the average of around the middle of the pack—which signaled to the model a notable dip in later academic performance compared to typical successful students. Next in influence was your LSAT score, which the model saw as a significant shortfall relative to the higher scores common among those who pass the bar, further tilting the prediction toward failure. Your Year 1 decile of 1 also contributed by reinforcing an early pattern of lower grades against the stronger starts seen in most passers. While these academic indicators dominated the decision—each nudging the model toward expecting a bar failure—your full-time status aligned with the vast majority of students (over 90%), so it didn't sway things much either way. In contrast to typical admitted students, who often show mid-to-high deciles and LSATs closer to 37, your metrics painted a riskier picture for bar passage. This isn't a judgment on your potential or background, but a data-driven call based on patterns from thousands of past law students. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score toward the average, strengthening your later-year grades through targeted prep or additional coursework, and exploring programs with bar passage support tailored for diverse applicants like yourself. Reapplying after addressing these gaps could shift the model's view in your favor, and many students in similar situations succeed by focusing on these exact areas. Keep pushing—your determination as a Black man pursuing law is already a strength worth building on.
